sub: Feasibility first — connect real hardware to your own Java code. Then the optional deep dive.

title: The Mission: A Feasibility Test
desc: Can we control a DJI drone with our own Java code? Prove the connection works — nothing more.

items: Build your own app that connects to the drone and delivers at least one first sign of life — e.g. reading the battery level
Explicitly out of scope: no flying, no automation — this is purely about whether the connection works at all
Document briefly where things get stuck — the error message and your hypothesis
These observations are not a byproduct: they form the backbone of the later lecture

body.card: Why this matters for the course
items: Everything in Modules 5–13 (UTM, APIs, streaming, testing) assumed the drone is reachable by software — this lab checks that assumption against real hardware
The SDK's capabilities and limits define what a university drone project can realistically build on

title: Shopping List: Hardware That Is Actually Programmable
desc: The single most expensive mistake is buying a drone without SDK support

rows: Neo, Neo 2 or Mini 5 Pro — these have no SDK and are not programmable
RC-N3 — the screenless variant you clamp a phone into
RC 2 with built-in display — it locks out third-party apps
An Android phone for development; must fit the holder: max. 180 × 86 × 10 mm

items: The Android phone may already be available — check before buying
General rule for any DJI project: verify SDK support first, model names alone tell you nothing

title: Set Up the Toolchain
desc: Four downloads, one account — everything is free

rows: Create a DJI developer account and generate an App Key
Mobile SDK tutorial · Java API reference (v5)

title: First Contact: Registration & Battery
desc: Success = 'registration success' + one real value on screen

items: Open the sample project in Android Studio and enter your App Key
Most common failure: the App Key must exactly match the app's package name — otherwise registration fails
Build the app on a real Android device — the emulator does not work
Pair drone and remote controller, connect the phone to the controller via USB, start the app
The app reports "registration success" and recognizes the connected drone
First real test: read a value — e.g. battery percentage via the KeyManager — and display it in the app
Write down where it gets stuck: which step, which error message — and ask when questions come up

title: Deep Dive A + B: Telemetry & the KeyManager
desc: Optional advanced track — the goal is understanding the SDK's architecture, not just 'it runs'

items: Don't stop at the battery level: use the KeyManager to subscribe to several values at once — position, altitude, attitude, satellite count, link status — and show them live in the app
Think along: what is the difference between a one-time get and a listen subscription — and when would you use which?

body.card: Level B — The KeyManager as a system
items: Map the key groups: FlightControllerKey, CameraKey, GimbalKey, BatteryKey, …
Build a small table: which values are read-only, which are also settable?
That table is the foundation for systematically capturing what the drone can do

title: Deep Dive C + D: Video Stream & First Control
desc: The didactic highlight — and the first, strictly simulated, control commands

items: Bring the camera video stream into your app and display it
This is the most exciting part didactically: computer vision builds on it later
Questions: in which format does the stream arrive, and what would it take to grab individual frames for image processing?
Before anything flies for real: use the DJI simulator
Send a simple virtual-stick command or a takeoff/landing sequence
Document which preconditions and safety mechanisms the SDK demands before it accepts control commands at all

title: The Guiding Question: Where Does the SDK End?

items: Concretely find out what does NOT work — e.g. direct access to the motors or the control loop
Naming this boundary cleanly is at least as valuable for the lecture as everything that works
Compare with Module 3: a consumer SDK enforcing safety boundaries is a trust-level decision — DJI does not let your code near the SIL-critical parts
Getting stuck is expected and welcome
Note the error message and your hypothesis about the cause
We will turn exactly these notes into a good exercise example later
